metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit `from_env()` wiring for the four `overlap_*` fields using `_llm_int`/appropriate helpers (e.g. `_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_K', 'overlap_k', 20)`) and extend the `cls(...)` constructor call in `from_env()` to include them.
findings.finding: `LLMConfig.from_env()` is not updated to wire the four new `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars into the dataclass fields. The plan adds `overlap_k`, `overlap_field_weights`, `overlap_max_doc_freq`, `overlap_min_should_match` to the `LLMConfig` dataclass and says they are readable via `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars and `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys — but the existing `from_env()` method (lines 457–524 of `src/rebar/llm/config.py`) constructs the dataclass with a hardcoded field list and has no mention of these new keys. The plan never calls out that `from_env` must be updated to resolve them.
findings.location: Config (LLMConfig, not VerifyConfig) section; src/rebar/llm/config.py lines 457-524
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/Config section, explicitly call out that `from_env()` must be updated: add `_llm_int`/`_llm_str` calls for `overlap_k`, `overlap_max_doc_freq`, `overlap_min_should_match`, and a JSON-parsed dict resolution for `overlap_field_weights`, then include those resolved values in the `return cls(...)` call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add `from_env()` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` to the edit-set, listing the four `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env-var reads to be wired; and strengthen `test_config` to verify env-var resolution (not just dataclass defaults).
findings.finding: `LLMConfig.from_env()` explicitly constructs `LLMConfig(...)` with all field values read via helpers; the plan adds four new fields to `LLMConfig` but doesn't enumerate updating `from_env()` to read the corresponding `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars and `[tool.rebar.llm]` table keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the wire format and resolution helper for the `overlap_field_weights` dict config (e.g., JSON-encoded env var parsed via a new `_llm_json_map` helper, or TOML-only with no env override) so `LLMConfig.from_env()` can be extended consistently.
findings.finding: The `overlap_field_weights` config key is a dict/map type, but `LLMConfig.from_env()` only defines `_llm_str` and `_llm_int` resolution helpers — no `_llm_dict`/`_llm_float_map` helper exists. The wiring protocol for this non-scalar config field is unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) consolidate the four overlap tunables into a single nested `overlap: OverlapConfig` dataclass (passed explicitly to `retrieve()`, not on `LLMConfig`) to avoid polluting the shared config surface, or (b) if keeping them on `LLMConfig`, specify how `overlap_field_weights` (dict) and `overlap_min_should_match`/`overlap_max_doc_freq` (float) are resolved from env/file in `from_env` — the existing `_llm_str`/`_llm_int` helpers do not cover these types.
findings.finding: Four new `LLMConfig` fields (`overlap_k`, `overlap_field_weights`, `overlap_max_doc_freq`, `overlap_min_should_match`) are added for a single sub-operation with zero existing consumers; two of the four types (float, dict[str,float]) have no corresponding resolver in `LLMConfig.from_env` (`_llm_str`/`_llm_int` only exist today), so honoring `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars and `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys would require new config-infrastructure for one feature.
findings.location: Config section — 'overlap_k (20), overlap_field_weights (the 3/3/1/1 map), overlap_max_doc_freq (0.5), overlap_min_should_match (0.15) — all on LLMConfig'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete type of `OverlapCandidate.matched_terms` (e.g., `set[str]` for a flat set of matched tokens, or `dict[str, list[str]]` for field-attributed terms) in the Scope/interface definition so the S7 contract is unambiguous.
findings.finding: `OverlapCandidate.matched_terms` field type is unspecified; S7 consumes this interface and the structural gap propagates downstream.
findings.location: What section — 'Returns a typed `OverlapCandidate` `[{ticket_id, score, matched_terms}]`'; Scope section — 'Interface for S7: `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude:set[str]) -> list[OverlapCandidate]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a TypedDict or inline type to the Scope section, e.g.: `class OverlapCandidate(TypedDict): ticket_id: str; score: float; matched_terms: set[str]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the interface contradiction: either add `ticket_id` and `tracker` to `retrieve()`'s signature (and update the Scope interface line) so it calls `related_ticket_ids` internally, or explicitly state the caller pre-computes `exclude` via `related_ticket_ids` and update the What/AC wording to match.
findings.finding: The `retrieve()` interface signature in Scope omits `ticket_id` and `tracker` parameters, yet the What section describes graph exclusion as happening *inside* `retrieve()` via `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)`, and the AC verifies it 'via `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)`'. If the caller is expected to pre-compute and pass `exclude:set[str]`, then `related_ticket_ids` lives outside `retrieve()` and the AC test `test_graph_exclusion` should test the caller pipeline, not `retrieve()` alone. If `related_ticket_ids` is called inside `retrieve()`, the interface needs `ticket_id` and `tracker` parameters. The two sections send the implementer in opposite directions.
findings.location: Scope (interface signature `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude:set[str])`) vs What ('Graph exclusion BEFORE scoring' calling `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)`) vs AC2 ('Candidates EXCLUDE … via `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)`')
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one canonical design and make all three sections consistent. Recommended: add `ticket_id: str, tracker` to `retrieve()`'s signature so graph exclusion is fully encapsulated — update Scope to `retrieve(ticket_id, query_digest, corpus, tracker) -> list[OverlapCandidate]` and remove `exclude` as a separate parameter, since the What section already describes building it internally.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit assertion to `test_graph_exclusion` (or a dedicated sub-case) that verifies a ticket whose `deps` list *names the query ticket* (incoming direction) is also excluded — i.e. set up a corpus fixture where ticket B has `query_ticket` in its `deps`, then confirm B is absent from the returned candidates.
findings.finding: The test_graph_exclusion AC lists 'linked dropped' but does not explicitly require asserting the INCOMING direction — where ticket B names query_ticket in B's own deps (a blocks/depends_on edge originating from B). The plan's What section calls this out as a specific non-obvious correctness requirement ('outgoing-only would miss it'), but the test specification doesn't mandate a fixture or assertion that confirms incoming linked tickets are excluded.
findings.location: Testing section — `test_graph_exclusion`; also Acceptance Criteria bullet 2; also What section ('incoming blocks/depends_on from another ticket is not in this ticket\'s own deps, so outgoing-only would miss it')
findings.suggested_fix: In the test, create two fixtures: (a) query_ticket has ticket A in its own deps (outgoing link) and (b) ticket B has query_ticket in its deps (incoming link). Assert both A and B are absent from retrieve() results. This directly validates the bidirectional scan described in graph.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify 'flattening its type-bucketed id lists' to name exactly the four type-bucket keys to flatten: `epics + stories + tasks + bugs` (omitting `parents_with_children`, which is an auxiliary key, not a type bucket).
findings.finding: The plan instructs 'flattening its type-bucketed id lists' from `list_descendants` output, but `list_descendants` returns FIVE keys: `epics`, `stories`, `tasks`, `bugs` (the four type buckets), AND `parents_with_children` — an auxiliary list of intermediate nodes that also appear in the type buckets. Naïve flattening of all five keys would double-count intermediate parent descendants.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'flattening its type-bucketed id lists' with 'collecting `result["epics"] + result["stories"] + result["tasks"] + result["bugs"]` (the four type-bucket lists; `parents_with_children` is auxiliary and should be omitted)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which keys from the `list_descendants()` return value are unioned into the exclusion set — specify explicitly whether `parents_with_children` is included alongside the four type buckets.
findings.finding: `list_descendants()` returns five keys including `parents_with_children`; the plan says 'flatten its type-bucketed id lists' but does not specify whether `parents_with_children` is included in the exclusion set, leaving the flattening decision rule ambiguous.
findings.location: What section — Graph exclusion: 'by calling `descendants.list_descendants(ticket_id, tracker)` and flattening its type-bucketed id lists'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the descendants flattening note: 'Union all five keys: `epics | stories | tasks | bugs | parents_with_children`' (or explicitly state only the four type buckets if intermediate parents are intentionally allowed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a timeout (or deadline propagation) to the `_load_all_states` call inside `related_ticket_ids`, and document how a timeout is surfaced (e.g., raises `TimeoutError` so the catch-all handler can return `[]` within budget).
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for the `_load_all_states(tracker)` I/O call inside `related_ticket_ids`. A slow or hung state store will not raise an exception, so the catch-all exception handler cannot rescue the caller — `retrieve()` (and by extension `review_plan`) will block indefinitely rather than degrading gracefully.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `_load_all_states(tracker)` call with a timeout mechanism (e.g., `concurrent.futures` with a short deadline, or a context-level timeout if the underlying store supports it). Ensure the timeout raises an exception so the existing catch-all handler can return `[]` and log the failure within the 10 ms latency budget.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide whether `_load_all_states` should be promoted to a public export in `descendants.py` (or a shared reads module) so `graph.py` can import it without relying on a private symbol across package boundaries; update the plan to reflect the chosen approach.
findings.finding: `graph.py` imports the private `_load_all_states` from `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` across package boundaries without the plan acknowledging the private-symbol boundary or proposing to expose it.
findings.location: What > Graph exclusion section: 'loads states ONCE via `_load_all_states(tracker)` (the same helper `descendants.py` uses)'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename `_load_all_states` → `load_all_states` in `descendants.py` and update the plan's edit-set to include that rename; or document the private import as intentional with a stability note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or address) that `_load_all_states` drops `error`/`fsck_needed` tickets, so inbound links FROM such tickets are silently missed; either document this as an accepted limitation or supplement with a byte-level scan (as `find_inbound_relationships` does) for the inbound-link half of the exclusion set.
findings.finding: The plan's inbound-link detection scans all tickets' `deps` using the `_load_all_states`-derived dict, but `_load_all_states` silently omits tickets with status `error` or `fsck_needed`. An errored ticket that has declared a `depends_on` or `blocks` targeting the query ticket will be absent from the dict, so that inbound link is silently dropped from the exclusion set — the plan claims BOTH-direction coverage but delivers it only for non-error tickets.
findings.location: What — Graph exclusion section: 'linked tickets in BOTH directions (scan all tickets\u2019 deps for any edge whose source OR target is ticket_id')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Inline the freshness-check predicate signature (field names from the digest record that must be compared, and what they are compared against) in the 'Cold/stale' spec bullet, so the implementer of `retrieve.py` need not cross-reference S1/S2.
findings.finding: The freshness-check protocol (what fields constitute staleness, where the check is performed, what comparator value is used) is deferred entirely to S2 without being co-located with the `retrieve()` schema that applies it.
findings.location: What section — 'Cold/stale: corpus tickets whose digest is absent or stale (S2 freshness: content-hash/model/version mismatch — not an age threshold) are SKIPPED'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing: the BM25F-over-ANN decision rationale, the field-weight defaults and their empirical basis, the graph-exclusion contract, and the S7 interface signature — so the design intent is durable beyond the ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage (Stage-1 overlap retrieval) with a novel BM25F architecture, a new cross-component graph-traversal helper, and a defined S7 interface contract — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry is planned to capture this architectural decision for future maintainers.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-overlap-stage1-bm25f.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section under `src/rebar/llm/overlap/`) covering: (1) why brute-force BM25F not ANN/vector-DB, (2) field weights defaults and spike evidence, (3) graph-exclusion semantics and the four relation sets, (4) the `retrieve()` interface contract for S7, (5) the 'next_batch.py not refactored' scope boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `related_ticket_ids` needs its own module now (Rule-of-Three: name ≥2 additional planned call-sites) or can live inline in `retrieve.py` until a second consumer exists.
findings.finding: `graph.py::related_ticket_ids` is extracted into its own module with exactly one named call-site (`retrieve.py`); S7 consumes `retrieve()` not this helper directly, and no other consumer is named. Rule-of-Three is not met for a separate module.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the graph-exclusion logic inline into `retrieve.py` (a private `_related_ids` function); promote to `graph.py` only when a second consumer materialises.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` to the plan's edit-set (even if empty) so the new directory is a proper Python package.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py` and `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py` as new files but omits `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py`, which is required to make the directory a Python package importable by test code and future consumers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` as an empty file to the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and cite the specific `rank-bm25` version/API reference confirming it lacks per-field BM25F weighting, and confirm no other lightweight library (e.g. `bm25s`) provides field-weighted BM25F at n<1000 scale before committing to the bespoke implementation.
findings.finding: The plan's sole justification for the bespoke BM25F implementation over the `rank-bm25` library is the assertion that 'rank-bm25 provides only UNWEIGHTED BM25' — but no version, documentation link, or changelog reference is cited to verify this claim. If a future version of rank-bm25 adds per-field weighting, or if an alternative library (e.g. `bm25s`, which supports field-level scoring via pre-tokenized arrays) already provides it, the bespoke-vs-library decision rests on an unverified foundation.
findings.location: What section — 'Bespoke ~40 LOC: `rank-bm25` provides only UNWEIGHTED BM25, but we need per-field weighting'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the 'What' section: e.g. '`rank-bm25` (≤0.2.2) exposes `BM25Okapi/Plus/L` — all document-level, no per-field `k1`/`b`/weight tuning; `bm25s` offers array scoring but not multi-field BM25F natively — bespoke remains the clearest fit at n<1000.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `deps` array entry schema (`{target_id, relation}`) and the exhaustive `relation` vocabulary (`blocks`, `depends_on`) inline in the graph-exclusion spec, so `graph.py` implementers do not need to read `next_batch.py` to get the struct right.
findings.finding: `deps` entry schema and `relation` vocabulary are referenced but never defined in the plan, leaving the bidirectional link-scan protocol structurally underspecified.
findings.location: What section — Graph exclusion: 'scan all tickets' deps for any edge whose source OR target is ticket_id — an incoming blocks/depends_on from another ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Graph exclusion' bullet: 'Each `deps` entry has shape `{target_id: str, relation: str}`; relation values are `"blocks"` and `"depends_on"`. To find tickets linked TO `ticket_id`, scan every other ticket's `deps` for entries where `target_id == ticket_id`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
